EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and sub-zero temperatures blanketed southeastern Wyoming Valentine's Day weekend. Southern mountains and portions out in the high plains measured heavy snowfall coupled then with extreme sub-zero temperatures into the minus teens and twenties for many. Rawlins and Laramie set new daily record low temperatures. With area ranchers in calving season, many had to prepare additional feed and wind breaks to keep herd alive along with snow during portion of the extreme cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Laramie Airport along with Union Pacific Railroad and Wyoming Department of Transportation sensors measured wind chills below minus 30. In addition, Laramie Airport also set a new record low temperature for the fourteenth with minus 17 breaking the previous record of minus 14 set back in 1951. With area ranchers in calving season, many had to prepare additional feed and wind breaks to keep herd alive along with snow during portion of the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall and slick conditions on Interstate 80 contributed to a tractor-trailer driver to lose control of their vehicle on the morning of April 9th just west of Laramie. Wyoming State Highway Patrol investigating if driver's speed was contributing factor.

Wyoming State Highway Patrol investigating if driver's speed was contributing factor. Area CoCoRaHs observers measured around Laramie half of inch of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a couple of fronts moving across the region, a strong jet over Wyoming and strong boundary layer winds mixing to the surface brought a period of high winds to portions of central Wyoming. The highest winds were in and in the lee of the Absarokas. Winds along with Chief Joseph Highway gusted as high as 88 mph. In the Cody Foothills, Clark, Wyoming had winds gusts as high as 87 mph with 61 mph gusts at the Cody, Wyoming Airport. Wind gusted as high as 72 mph south of Casper, Wyoming. In Riverton, Wyoming, strong winds tore the roof off an auto repair shop. Wind gusts over 60 mph were also reported in Johnson and Sweetwater Counties in central and southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor along the Chief Joseph Highway had a prolonged period of high wind. The maximum wind gust reported was 88 mph.

The highest winds were in and in the lee of the Absarokas. Winds along with Chief Joseph Highway gusted as high as 88 mph. In the Cody Foothills, Clark, Wyoming had winds gusts as high as 87 mph with 61 mph gusts at the Cody, Wyoming Airport. Wind gusted as high as 72 mph south of Casper, Wyoming. In Riverton, Wyoming, strong winds tore the roof off an auto repair shop. Wind gusts over 60 mph were also reported in Johnson and Sweetwater Counties in central and southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The periods of high wind occurred in eastern Sweetwater County, Wyoming, the first on the 21st and the second of the 23rd. The maximum wind gust reported was 61 mph at the Sweetwater County, Wyoming airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front moved through Wyoming and brought heavy snow to portions of the area. The Tetons had the heaviest snow with 20 inches of Grand Targhee and 17 inches at Jackson Hole. Heavy snow also fell in portions of the Salt and Wyoming Range, Star Valley and Jackson Valley. Meanwhile, convective snow banding developed in southern Hot Springs and northern Fremont Counties on the 27th. At Boysen Dam, 10 inches of new snow was measured. Amounts dropped sharply outside of that with other areas receiving less than 3 inches. Ahead of the front, strong winds occurred in the wind prone areas around Casper, Wyoming. The maximum wind gust was 68 mph along Wyoming Boulevard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Tetons. The Grand Targhee Ski Resort had 20 inches of new snow and 17 inches fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first, widespread winter storm occurred across most of the Billings Forecast Area from February 5th through February 7th. This was also the first and only arctic airmass of the season that impacted the Billings Forecast Area.||An arctic front blasted through the area on the 5th. Following the front, a moist Pacific flow aloft moved over this arctic airmass which resulted in a prolonged period of heavy snow.  After the snow ended and skies cleared, overnight lows of -20F to -40F were common. Many areas stayed below zero for 7 consecutive days.||Major impacts occurred with this storm as warm roads initially melted the snow. These wet roads then quickly froze resulting in icy and hazardous driving conditions. As a result, many accidents occurred across the area. In addition to the snow, strong winds resulted in blowing and drifting snow, as well as significantly reduced visibilities. I-90 from the Montana border south to Buffalo, Wyoming was closed due to severe driving conditions. Sheridan reported half mile visibilities in snow with wind gusts to 38 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 inches occurred across the area. In addition, I-90 was closed due to severe driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early May heavy snowfall was observed at the Webber Springs SNOTEL sensor in the Sierra Madre Range where 1.30 inches of snow water equivalent was measured and applying a ten to one ratio gave an estimated snowfall total of 13 inches.

A strong ridge parked over the northern Rockies and brought very hot temperatures to much of western and central Wyoming. All of the lower elevations saw high temperatures 90 degrees or above with many high temperatures over 100 in the lower elevations East of the Continental Divide. The Greybull and Johnson County airports tied their all time record highs of 108 and 104 degrees respectively, on June 15th. The high of 106 at the Worland airport on June 15th missed the all time record high by 1 degree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well above normal temperatures occurred in Yellowstone Park. The highest temperature was 91 degrees at Mammoth Hot Springs on June 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level moisture moved into Wyoming on June 8th. Dry air at the surface led to large dew point depressions at the surface. Only weak convective showers were noted on radar, but some showers and a mesoscale frontal feature produced strong winds. The strongest wind was at Greybull, Wyoming, where a gust of 79 mph occurred. The strong winds caused damage to a roof and a house under construction. A large tree was uprooted and a camper was tipped over. Strong wind also occurred at Lander and Buffalo, Wyoming, which saw wind gusts of 59 and 58 mph, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Lander, Wyoming airport reported a wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An combination of favorable jet position, a cold frontal passage and strong mid level winds mixing to the surface brought high winds to much of the state. The epicenter of the wind was in Park County, especially near Cody, Wyoming. A wind gust of 84 mph was reported north of Cody, Wyoming, with a gust of 80 mph to the south. Numerous branches and several trees were downed by the wind, causing numerous power outages. On Highway 120, six trucks were blown over by the high wind. The rodeo in Cody, Wyoming was cancelled for the first time in at least 17 years due to the wind. Wind gusts over 58 mph were also reported in Fremont, Johnson, Lincoln, Natrona and Sweetwater Counties in central and southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph just was reported at the Natrona County airport. A 60 mph wind gust was reported near Hat Six road to the east of Casper, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms across Laramie and Niobrara Counties produced severe hail over 1 inch in diameter. For the Laramie County storm, prolonged training and heavy rain produced flash flooding across portions of the city of Cheyenne with hail accumulating to depths of several inches deep. An NWS employee on the north side of town noted animal loss from the hail and a greenhouse damaged by the wind driven hail.

A significant wind event occurred across the area from the early morning through the late day ahead of and behind a strong cold front. Strong west to southwest winds developed during the early morning hours across parts of northeastern Wyoming, with gusts near 60 mph were recorded in Campbell County. The strong cold front then moved through during the mid to late morning hours, followed by sustained winds over 40 mph and wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph from the northwest through the early afternoon.

Lee cyclogenesis occurred over Colorado and low pressure moved slowly into the Plains states. Favorable east to northeasterly upslope flow brought heavy snow to portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Casper area. Mesoscale banding developed in the area and brought extremely heavy snowfall rates on the morning of March 14th. A total of 15 inches of snow fell between 6 am and noon. There were widespread reports of over 15 inches of snow around Casper with a total storm total of 26.3 inches at the Casper Airport. This total was the 3rd largest storm total snowfall in station history and the largest storm total since December 1982. Heavy snow also fell in portions of Fremont, Sweetwater and Johnson Counties. The heavy snow closed many major roads, including Interstates 25 and 80 as well as US 20 26 and US 287. Some closures stretched for 3 days, stranding many travelers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and gusty winds led to the closure of many roads around the area, including US 287.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong cold front moving across Wyoming and very strong low level winds mixing to the surface brought a widespread high wind event to much of western and central Wyoming. The highest wind gusts reported was 92 mph along the Chief Joseph Highway. The most noteworthy gust was at the Natrona County airport near Casper. The wind gust of 90 mph was the all time record. Wind gusts of hurricane force were also reported in Fremont, Teton and Fremont Counties. All counties reported wind gusts past 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations in the Wind River Mountains had wind gusts past 74 mph. The highest recorded gust was 91 mph at the Red Canyon sensor along Highway 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent moist flow of Pacific moisture, the passage of several shortwaves and favorable jet dynamics brought heavy snow to much of Teton County. The heaviest snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort where three day amounts ranged from 19 inches at the base to 38 inches at the summit. Heavy snow fell in the Jackson Valley as well with amounts anywhere from 9 to 16 inches commonplace.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Tetons. The highest 3 day total was 38 inches at the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extreme windy set of days were felt across southeastern Wyoming in the middle of January as a sharp frontal boundary and pressure gradient produced high winds well in excess of 58 mph with several locations measuring gusts into the 80s and 90 mph ranges. Several reports of blown over semi trailers on Interstate 80 and 25 resulted in the roadway being closed for a period of time along with area restrictions for light, high profile vehicles. Winds caused upwards of $30000 in damages across the City of Torrington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor near Shirley Rim along Wyoming Highway 487 measured the reported wind gust.

Damage estimate by emergency manager between $25k-30k.

Winds caused upwards of $30000 in damages across the City of Torrington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cheyenne Airport sensor and a Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Interstate 25 at Bear Creek or eight miles south of Chugwater both measured the reported wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall accumulation were measured by various SNOTEL sensors across the Snowy and Sierra Madre Ranges from late on the fourth through the afternoon of the 5th. North French Creek sensor in the Snowy Range measured an estimated 19.5 inches of snowfall. In the Sierra Madre Range,  Old Battle SNOTEL measured an estimated 18 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North French Creek sensor at an elevation of 10130 feet measured a snow water equivalent of 1.3 inches and applying a snow to liquid ratio of 15 to 1 gave an estimated snowfall total of 19.5 inches. Med Bow sensor also measured an estimated 16.5 inches of snowfall with 1.1 inches snow water equivalent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 13, 2021, a particularly strong wind event impacted the entire Billings Forecast Area with strong, damaging winds, not only along the foothills (where strong winds are common), but also across the lower elevations of south central and southeast Montana, as well as northern Wyoming. These strong winds were associated with a cold frontal passage and lasted for several hours.||The arrival of a Pacific cold front on the 13th was followed by a period of subsidence and very strong winds across the lower elevations. Aloft, a strong northwesterly oriented jet stream was driving through the northern Rockies, resulting in strong winds at mountain top level. In addition, the cold front also produced light shower activity which enhanced the gustiness of the surface winds. The combination of all these ingredients resulted in the very strong winds. Westerly, wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph were common, and gusts exceeded 90 mph at some mountain sites. Sheridan Airport reported a gust of 67 mph which was the 4th highest January gust. (Please see event summary for south central and southeast Montana for details of this event).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 74 mph just northwest of Ranchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept across Wyoming on January third. Ahead of the front, high winds occurred in some of the favored areas in Natrona and Johnson Counties. Winds gusted to 72 mph near Clark, Wyoming and 62 mph to the south of Casper, Wyoming along Wyoming Boulevard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 72 mph wind gust was recorded 8 miles south-southwest of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved across Wyoming. At the same time, strong mid level winds and favorable jet dynamics brought high winds to many parts of western and central Wyoming. The highest wind gust reported was 101 mph at the weather sensor at Red Canyon along Highway 28 in the Wind River Mountains. In the lower elevations, some of the more notable gusts included 70 mph at Buffalo, Wyoming,  68 mph at Riverton, Wyoming and  68 mph near Casper, Wyoming. The strong winds caused the closure of many roads to high profile vehicles during the height of the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred in the Absarokas. A maximum wind gust of 83 mph was reported along the Chief Joseph Highway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow averaging six inches according to plow drivers between Muddy Gap and Rawlins on Highway 287 and 789.

A clipper system moved across the region, bringing accumulating snowfall to the Wyoming Black Hills and Bear Lodge Mountains. A prolonged period of occasional upslope-enhanced snow brought three to seven inches of snow to most of the area over an 18 to 24 hour period.

High winds were felt across the North Snowy Range Foothills at Wyoming Department of Transportation (WYDOT) sensors along Interstate 80. Highest wind gust was 64 mph at the Arlington site. WYDOT had reported various crashes during the morning hours of the 28th with drifting snow being reported and travel restrictions to light and high profile vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation (WYDOT) sensor along Interstate 80 at Arlington measured the reported wind gust. WYDOT reported various crashes during the morning hours of the 28th with drifting snow being reported and travel restrictions to light and high profile vehicles. No additional information regarding any injuries or damage related to crashes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable, northwest flow aloft resulted in heavy snow along the Sheridan Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dayton and Big Horn both reported 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized high wind occurred in eastern Natrona County. Wind gusts at Hat Six Road to the east of Casper peaked at 58 mph.
A fast moving system brought heavy snow to portions of the Tetons. At the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, 12 inches of new snow fell.

An extreme windy set of days were felt across southeastern Wyoming in the middle of January as a sharp frontal boundary and pressure gradient produced high winds well in excess of 58 mph with several locations measuring gusts into the 80s and 90 mph ranges. Several reports of blown over semi trailers on Interstate 80 and 25 resulted in the roadway being closed for a period of time along with area restrictions for light, high profile vehicles. Winds caused upwards of $30000 in damages across the City of Torrington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Douglas Airport sensor measured the reported wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a Pacific Cold Front and an ample supply of moisture brought heavy snow to western Wyoming. In the mountains, the highest snowfall total was 28 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Around 20 inches of snow was the maximum amount in the Salt and Wyoming Range. In the western Valleys, over a foot of snow fell in portions of the Star Valley with up to 10 inches in the northern Jackson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout the Tetons. The highest amount measured was 28 inches on top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Salt and Wyoming Range. Some of the highest amounts included 21 inches at the Box Y Ranch and at Commissary Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The next in a series of Pacific troughs and ample moisture brought another round of heavy snow to portions of the western mountains and the Star Valley. The heaviest snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort where 30 inches of new snow fell. Amounts elsewhere were generally under 15 inches. In the Salt and Wyoming Range, 12 to 18 inches was common. In the lower elevations, in the Star Valley, amounts ranged from 7 to 14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Tetons. At the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, 30 inches of new snow fell. Amounts elsewhere were generally 15 inches or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another Pacific cold front brought another round of moderate to heavy snow to the western mountains. The highest amounts once again fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort where 29 inches of new snow fell. In the Jackson Valley, amounts ranged from around 6 inches in Jackson to around a foot near Moose. The Star Valley also saw heavy snow in the northern portions with a foot of snow near Alpine. Amounts dropped further south with only 4 inches near Afton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Jackson Valley. The highest amount recorded was 29 inches on top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of 6 snowboarders built a jump on a bank near Togwotee Pass. The first person to go off the jump triggered a slab avalanche that buried him 9 feet deep in the snow. The man was wearing a transceiver and was eventually excavated.  The man was given CPR but died at the scene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see episode narrative for details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 33 year old snowboarder was riding down steep terrain in the Tetons. He triggered an avalanche that carried the snowboarder over a 50 foot cliff into steep and rocky, terrain, He did not survive the fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see episode narrative for details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic boundary slowly sagged southward across much of northern and central Wyoming and brought bitterly cold temperatures. Temperatures in northern Wyoming fell as low as minus 30. In addition, a couple of moisture laden troughs moved across the area and brought another round of heavy snow to western Wyoming. Some parts of Yellowstone Park had as much as 30 inches of snow. Other locations in Teton, Lincoln and Sublette County also received over a foot of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of Yellowstone Park. Over an foot of snow fell in many locations.

A line of severe thunderstorms developed over far northeastern Wyoming. Wind gusts over 60 mph developed at Beulah.

Three thunderstorms across portions of Carbon, Converse and Niobrara Counties measured thunderstorm wind gusts in excess of 60 mph. Strong inverted V model soundings were present for the area with overall decent CAPE values for development. These storms struggled to register over 50 dBZ echoes from the KCYS radar though given proximity for these storms from the radar, any base echoes were already over 10000 feet above the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds measured at the Rawlins Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic winter storm crippled much of southeast Wyoming with heavy wet snow followed by strong winds and blizzard conditions. Cheyenne received 22.7 inches of snowfall in 24 hours (storm total 30.8 inches) with 4 to 6 foot drifts developing all over. Highest snowfall amount reported over 70 inches (4.5 inches snow water equivalent) in the North Laramie Range. Most areas were digging out from the storm for at least the following week as snow plows got stuck on roadways. Received numerous reports of roof collapses due to the heavy, wet snow. Areas of southeast Wyoming also experienced thundersnow with the heavier snow bands early Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Former NWS employee reported blizzard conditions most of the day Sunday with visibility below one eighth mile and strong wind gusts around 40 MPH 8 miles southwest of Wheatland. Reported average snow depth around 3 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation mesonet station WY1 Coleman along Interstate 25 reported wind gusts 35 to 55 MPH with one quarter mile visibility. Area webcams showed whiteout conditions. Interstate 25 Wheatland to Douglas was closed starting 8:15 PM MST March 13th until 8:45 AM MDT March 17th.

Officially storm total at NWS Cheyenne office was 30.8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lee cyclogenesis occurred over Colorado and low pressure moved slowly into the Plains states. Favorable east to northeasterly upslope flow brought heavy snow to portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Casper area. Mesoscale banding developed in the area and brought extremely heavy snowfall rates on the morning of March 14th. A total of 15 inches of snow fell between 6 am and noon. There were widespread reports of over 15 inches of snow around Casper with a total storm total of 26.3 inches at the Casper Airport. This total was the 3rd largest storm total snowfall in station history and the largest storm total since December 1982. Heavy snow also fell in portions of Fremont, Sweetwater and Johnson Counties. The heavy snow closed many major roads, including Interstates 25 and 80 as well as US 20 26 and US 287. Some closures stretched for 3 days, stranding many travelers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Favorable northeasterly upslope flow brought heavy snow to the eastern slopes of the Wind River Range. The highest snow total was 26 inches at the Hobbs Park SNOTEL site. A combination of heavy snow and strong wind led to the closure of Wyoming 28 over South Pass for many hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong cold front moving across Wyoming and very strong low level winds mixing to the surface brought a widespread high wind event to much of western and central Wyoming. The highest wind gusts reported was 92 mph along the Chief Joseph Highway. The most noteworthy gust was at the Natrona County airport near Casper. The wind gust of 90 mph was the all time record. Wind gusts of hurricane force were also reported in Fremont, Teton and Fremont Counties. All counties reported wind gusts past 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph wind gust was reported at Clark, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic winter storm crippled much of southeast Wyoming with heavy wet snow followed by strong winds and blizzard conditions. Cheyenne received 22.7 inches of snowfall in 24 hours (storm total 30.8 inches) with 4 to 6 foot drifts developing all over. Highest snowfall amount reported over 70 inches (4.5 inches snow water equivalent) in the North Laramie Range. Most areas were digging out from the storm for at least the following week as snow plows got stuck on roadways. Received numerous reports of roof collapses due to the heavy, wet snow. Areas of southeast Wyoming also experienced thundersnow with the heavier snow bands early Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rawlins Municipal Airport ASOS station KRWL reported 35 to 40 MPH wind gusts and quarter mile visibility for 3 hours. Area webcams showed whiteout conditions. 8 to 12 inches of snowfall reported around Rawlins.

Easterly to southeasterly near surface flow, to the cool side of a zone of stronger differential surface heating and deeper boundary-layer mixing to the east of the higher terrain, resulted in sufficient boundary-layer moisture to contribute to CAPE|on the order of 1000-1500 J/kg.  In addition, a belt of 40+kt northwesterly flow around |500mb resulted in strong deep-layer shear. As a result, numerous severe thunderstorms producing large hail developed across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved into the state, bring abnormally high precipitable water levels. Thunderstorms formed and moved slowly within very light steering flow, with some storms becoming nearly stationary. At Rock Springs, over two inches of rain fell in under an hour, producing flooding across western portions of the city. Several roads were flooded and were impassable for hours. The force of the flowing water was enough to break basement windows. Numerous houses had up to six feet of water in basements with damage of 30 to 40 thousand dollars at some homes. The Rock Springs city council declared a disaster and state of emergency. To date, 59 people notified the council that their homes were effected by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from higher desert terrain and rain falling on an urbanized landscape led to flash flooding across western portions of Rock Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a cold front, a potent jet, and strong winds being mixed to the surface brought high winds to much of northern and central Wyoming. The highest wind speeds were near the foothills of the Absaroka Range, where wind gusts as high as 120 mph occurred to the west of Clark. These intense gusts downed a power line, which was responsible for igniting a wildfire in the Line Creek drainage north of Clark. The wildfire spread quickly and destroyed several structures and caused one death. Wind gusts to around 90 mph were reported in northern Johnson County downing power lines that sparked several small grass fires. Wind gusts over 58 mph were also reported in Fremont and Hot Springs counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gap flow coupled with a mountain wave developed around Clark and brought extremely strong wind gusts for several hours on the evening of Monday, November 15. A maximum wind gust of 120 mph was reported to the west of Clark at 2100MST. A wind gust of 75 mph was measured at the Clark firehouse around midnight November 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds for southeast Wyoming wind prone areas led to Interstate 80 between Laramie and Rawlins being closed to light, high-profile vehicles for much of the late morning and afternoon on the 23rd with gusts to 66 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple WYDOT mesonet stations reported high winds including at Elk Mountain (WY19) along Interstate 80 where the maximum wind gust was measured. Interstate 80 between Laramie and Rawlins was closed to light, high-profile vehicles for 7 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific Cold Front and associated trough moved across the Cowboy State and brought heavy snow and high wind to portions of the area. The snow and wind disrupted holiday travel across the affected areas. The heaviest snow fell in Yellowstone National Park where the higher elevations received up to two feet of new snow. Over a foot of snow also fell in portions of the Teton and Wyoming mountain ranges. The highest amount in the lower elevations was 11 inches near Wilson in the Jackson Valley. High winds also occurred east of the Continental Divide. Ahead of the front, the wind prone areas south of Casper saw wind gusts as high as 68 mph. Behind the front, a wind gust to 59 mph was registered at the airport near Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Teton Range. The highest total was 16 inches at the Grand Targhee SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short period of moderate to heavy snow fell in the high mountains of southeast Wyoming from late in the afternoon on the 16th through the early morning hours on the 17th. Snowfall totals were estimated to range from 6 to 10 inches, highest at elevations above 9000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow occurred over the Sierra Madre mountains with the heaviest snow observed mainly above 9000 feet elevation. Snow Water Equivalent values of 0.3 to 0.5 inches with ratios near 20 to 1 suggest estimated totals of 6 to 10 inches at multiple SNOTEL sites including Old Battle (OLDW4) and Little Snake River (LITW4). Precipitation accumulation data suggests the heaviest precipitation fell late on the 16th into the early morning hours on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a cold front, a potent jet, and strong winds being mixed to the surface brought high winds to much of northern and central Wyoming. The highest wind speeds were near the foothills of the Absaroka Range, where wind gusts as high as 120 mph occurred to the west of Clark. These intense gusts downed a power line, which was responsible for igniting a wildfire in the Line Creek drainage north of Clark. The wildfire spread quickly and destroyed several structures and caused one death. Wind gusts to around 90 mph were reported in northern Johnson County downing power lines that sparked several small grass fires. Wind gusts over 58 mph were also reported in Fremont and Hot Springs counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong wind downed a power line into a tree igniting a wildfire around 2215MST Monday, November 15, in the Line Creek area north of Clark. The starting point appeared to be in the 800 block of County Road 1AB. The wildfire rapidly spread through the Line Creek drainage, mainly the north side of the creek, as well as the area around Gunpowder Road as westerly wind gusting between 60 and 80 mph drove the flames eastward. The blaze, which eventually burned at least 300 acres, destroyed three homes, seven outbuildings, and a bridge. The fire was also responsible for the death of a 61-year-old woman who succumbed to smoke inhalation on Tuesday, November 16. Firefighters responded from across northern Park County and southern Montana to get the fire under control. Evacuations occurred for Louis L'Amour Lane and Crossfire Trail.

These intense gusts downed a power line, which was responsible for igniting a wildfire in the Line Creek drainage north of Clark. The wildfire spread quickly and destroyed several structures and caused one death. Wind gusts to around 90 mph were reported in northern Johnson County downing power lines that sparked several small grass fires. Wind gusts over 58 mph were also reported in Fremont and Hot Springs counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds occurred in northern Johnson County. A wind sensor along I-90 near the Johnson-Sheridan county line recorded a maximum wind gust of 92 mph. The ASOS at Johnson County Airport near Buffalo registered a peak gust of 82 mph. The high winds downed several power lines that ignited small grass fires throughout the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of Pacific troughs crossed Wyoming and brought high wind and locally heavy snow to many areas. High wind occurred in Sweetwater and Natrona counties, where wind gusts of 70 and 58 mph were reported, respectively. A snow squall accompanied the cold front, producing wind gusts over 60 mph and heavy snow. This combination led to white-out conditions that closed Interstate 80 in Sweetwater County for a time. Heavy snow fell in Teton County where up to 13 inches was measured to the northwest of Jackson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer near Teton Village northwest of Jackson reported 13 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough moving across Wyoming, strong mid-level winds, and a potent jet combined to produce high wind across much of central Wyoming. The highest recorded wind was west of Clark where a maximum wind gust of 118 mph occurred. Wind gusts of 88 mph were observed around Casper destroying a horse barn at the fairgrounds. Wind gusts of hurricane force also occurred in the Green Mountains and Wind River Range. The high winds led to road closures at times as well as a ban on high-profile vehicles on many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 78 mph was registered at the Fales Rock RAWS site. Other notable wind gusts included 60 mph near Pathfinder Reservoir and 58 mph over Beaver Rim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very strong winds occurred for several days across the Billings Forecast Area. The strongest winds with accompanied damage occurred during the late evening of the 15th through the early afternoon hours on the 16th. A very strong jet stream overhead, along with rapid pressure rises behind a cold front, resulted in very strong, damaging winds. In addition, a mountain wave formed overnight over the Bighorn Mountains. This caused the strong jet stream winds aloft to be brought to the surface in an accelerated motion on the eastern side of the mountains. Story, Wyoming was hit the hardest as wind gusts to 96 mph were reported across that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 61 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds developed across the wind prone areas of southeast Wyoming during the afternoon on December 4th, with winds becoming progressively more widespread overnight through the early afternoon on December 5th as a strong cold front tracked across the area. A hybrid gap and bora event resulted in numerous wind gusts 65-75 MPH. WYDOT reported numerous closures to light weight and high profile vehicles along Interstate 80 between Rawlins and Cheyenne and along Interstate 25 between Wheatland and the Colorado State Line.

An upper-level low passed over Wyoming and spawned thunderstorms. Some of the storms combined with the gradient wind wrapping around the low to generate strong wind. At Lander, the wind accelerated downhill and produced hurricane force wind gusts at the airport in Lander. The wind downed numerous tree branches and limbs around the city. Thunderstorm outflow wind produced a peak gust of 64 mph at the Big Piney-Marbleton Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 79 mph was measured by the ASOS at Hunt Field in Lander. Numerous limbs were downed in the city of Lander. There was damage report to one large metal-sided structure on the west side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lack of any significant precipitation during the normally wet months of March, April and May, combined with above normal temperatures going into June resulted in the beginning of a significant drought across the Billings Forecast Area. Temperatures across many areas were around 100 degrees by the middle of June. Temperatures during July and August were mainly in the 90s and lower 100s. This combined with very little, if any rainfall, resulted in extreme drought conditions across the entire area throughout most of the summer, with the hardest hit areas across south central and southeast Montana, as well as, northern Wyoming. Wetter conditions resulted in some improvement during the middle and latter part of August.|The following are some of the impacts resulting from the drought: (For more details, please see https://droughtmonitor.unl.edu)||-The entire Billings Forecast Area was designated Extreme (D4) Drought Conditions.|-The period May-July was some of the driest periods on record with extreme heat.|-It was one of the top 5 warmest summers on record.|-High percentages of short/very short topsoil and subsoil moisture conditions|-Lowest 5-10th percentiles for many stream and river flows per USGS|-Low flows and warm temperatures resulted in fishing restrictions and closures per FWP|-Low crop yields (some of the lowest since 1988, some dating back to the 1930s)|-Hay shortages with many ranchers having to buy hay from out of state which was very|costly.|-Poor pasture land|-Significant grasshopper activity in places and associated losses|-Low water supplies for cattle|-Low weights in calves and cows|-Selling off calves and cows early|-Above normal wildfire activity, along with an early start to the wildfire season|-Fire restrictions prohibited campfires|-Poor air quality from local and western U.S. wildfires|-Firefighters facing physical and mental exhaustion due to the extensive nature of initial|attacks|-Agricultural producers dealing with the stress of the drought, some close to losing their|livelihoods due to their crop/livestock losses.|-Impacts to mental health.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Event Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and trough moved across Wyoming and brought high winds to portions of central Wyoming. The highest wind gust reported was 79 mph at Crowheart. Along Wyoming Boulevard south of Casper, several 65 mph wind gusts were registered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 MPH were measured along Interstate 80 near Arlington for over 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first snow occurred during the second week of October. A surface low moved northeast out of the Four Corners region across Wyoming into the northern high plains. This allowed cold air to move south out of Canada while drawing moisture northward from the Gulf of Mexico. However, most of the cold air was due to dynamic cooling as a result of this powerful low. Heavy snow occurred across the entire Billings Forecast Area with the heaviest occurring across southeast Montana, as well as northern Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area SNOTELS reported 12-18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid-October low brought heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions to southeast Wyoming. Areas west of the Laramie Range received 4 to 10 inches of snowfall with areas in the Snowy and Sierra Madres mountains receiving higher amounts. Precipitation remained light across Laramie County with around an inch of snowfall, but wind gusts approached 65 MPH. Farther north, heavier snowfall was observed with around 10 inches of snowfall mixed with strong winds resulting in whiteout blizzard conditions, especially in Converse and Niobrara Counties. Interstate 25 north of Wheatland was closed for around 12 hours due to winter conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WYDOT mesonet station WY1 Coleman along Interstate 25 measured 50 to 60 MPH wind gusts and one quarter mile visibility for over 3 hours. Interstate 25 between Wheatland and Douglas was closed for almost 12 hours. CoCoRaHS observers reported between 2 and 10 inches of snowfall.

Very dry conditions at the surface within a hot airmass generated large dewpoint depressions. As a result, dry microbursts and strong outflow wind occurred ahead of showers and thunderstorms, primarily across Fremont and Sweetwater counties. Winds gusted as high as 68 mph at Southwest Wyoming Regional Airport near Rock Springs and 60 to 65 mph in the vicinity of Lander and Riverton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Sand Creek Fire was first reported on Thursday, September 9, in the Sand Creek drainage about 13 miles west of Lander. The fire grew rapidly due to thunderstorm outflow wind and hot, dry conditions on Friday afternoon, September 10, expanding to nearly 750 acres by the morning of Saturday, September 11. The fire grew slowly over the next week, eventually encompassing 929 acres as 120 personnel assisted in suppressing the fire. Cooler and wetter weather moved in around Monday, September 20, with full containment achieved by the end of the month. The fire remained over the backcountry and caused no property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see episode for a description.

Areas west of the Laramie Range received 4 to 10 inches of snowfall with areas in the Snowy and Sierra Madres mountains receiving higher amounts. Precipitation remained light across Laramie County with around an inch of snowfall, but wind gusts approached 65 MPH. Farther north, heavier snowfall was observed with around 10 inches of snowfall mixed with strong winds resulting in whiteout blizzard conditions, especially in Converse and Niobrara Counties. Interstate 25 north of Wheatland was closed for around 12 hours due to winter conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 13.6 inches of snow accumulation at Sand Lake SNOTEL site (10050 feet elevation) with 0.8 inches of snow water equivalent and 17:1 snow ratios.

The first widespread winter storm of the season hit western and central Wyoming. The main effects from the storm were focused around central Wyoming. Areas favored by east to northeast flow received the most snow, especially in Johnson and Natrona counties. Areas near Buffalo received the most snow with up to 18 inches reported on the west side of town. In Natrona County, areas around Casper received anywhere from 10 to 18 inches, although amounts dropped substantially farther west. Amounts over 6 inches were also reported in Fremont and Hot Springs counties. In the higher elevations, the eastern slopes of the Bighorn Range had the most with up to 20 inches reported. The snow combined with a gusty wind to close many major roads for a time, including Interstate 25, US 20/26, and US 287.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout the eastern Bighorn Range. The highest amount was 20 inches at the Soldier Park SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds quickly returned to much of southeast Wyoming with gusts as high as 75 MPH being reported in Rawlins. Interstate 25 and 80 not only closed to light, high-profile vehicles, but also had to close due to winter conditions at times as a snow squall moved across Carbon and Albany Counties resulting in whiteout conditions. Additionally, the Snowy and Sierra Madre Mountains received 2 to 3 feet of snowfall on the 26th and 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: RAWS mesonet station Camp Creek (CAMW4) measured maximum wind gust 4 miles west of Muddy Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread strong winds developed across the wind prone areas of southeast Wyoming late in the evening on the 20th. In the early morning hours on the 21st, a severe mountain wave developed over the Southern Laramie Range, resulting in a sharp increase in wind speeds into the I-25 corridor including the city of Cheyenne. Sustained winds at the Cheyenne Airport reached nearly 60 MPH with gusts over 75 MPH for several hours with occasional gusts up to 65 MPH continuing well into the morning and afternoon on the 21st. Winds remained elevated for much of the area into the overnight hours, ending prior to sunrise on the 22nd. High winds resulted in road closures to light weight and high profile vehicles as well as minor tree and property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe mountain wave resulted in a prolonged period of extreme winds sustained at 50+ MPH with gusts up to 79 MPH at the Cheyenne (KCYS) ASOS, peaking at 3:30 AM MST. Winds remained strong through around 7:00 AM before decreasing slightly, but occasional gusts exceeding 60 MPH continued into the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred in the Sierra Madre Range, the Snowy Range, Upper North Platte River Basin and southwest Carbon County, beginning the 23rd of December and continued through 25 December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred in the Sierra Madre Range, the Snowy Range, Upper North Platte River Basin and southwest Carbon County, beginning the 23rd of December and continued through 25 December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the Arlington and Elk Mountains WYDOT sensors reported near blizzard conditions with visibility under one-half mile and winds gusting in excess of 45 mph. CoCoRaHS observed near Elk Mountain reported 3 inches of snow with icy road conditions underneath the snow at 10:30 AM on the 24th after the snow stopped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of very heavy snow occurred in the Snowy and Sierra Madre mountains with total estimated snowfall accumulations in excess of 2 feet observed at elevations above 9000 feet. Periods of light to moderate snow along with embedded snow squall structures affected lower elevation areas, mainly along and south of Interstate 80 in southeast Wyoming. The heaviest bands produced 3 to 4 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS COOP observer reported a 24 hour snowfall amount of 3.0 inches 1 mile north of Rawlins on the morning of the 31st.

Additionally, the Snowy and Sierra Madre Mountains received 2 to 3 feet of snowfall on the 26th and 27th.

The city of Cheyenne observed high winds when a mountain wave break resulted in 63 MPH winds around midnight on the 7th.

Interstate 80 between Laramie and Rawlins was closed to light, high-profile vehicles for 16 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow was observed across a large portion of southeast Wyoming on the 9th and 10th, with the heaviest snowfall generally observed to the north of a line from Laramie to Torrington. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level system tracked across much of northeastern Wyoming, bringing a swath of heavy snow to portions of the area. The heaviest snow bands developed across southern portions of northeastern Wyoming, especially Weston County, where six to 12 inches was reported. Amounts tapered off significantly to the north, with little or no snow over extreme northeastern Wyoming.
